feedback.text: Hello! This act seems like a really good idea, and I'm looking forward seeing it come to life. I'd like to give some suggestions tho. When it comes to digital contracts, that's an area with a lot of room for improvements. One thing that comes to mind immediately are EULAs. They are often presented after the purchase has been made, asking the consumer to accept them. This should be done beforehand, otherwise it's pressuring the user to agree to the terms, even though they might have given up on the purchase if they knew the details upfront. Secondly, EULAs ask the user to read them and give their consent, but they are often written in a legally technical way, and they are quite lengthy, so that the average user has no chance to understand what they're agreeing to. EULAs should only be allowed so that an average user can easily understand them. Finally, I want to lik this act with Stop Killing Games citizen's initiative, which has already accumulated over a million signatures, including mine. It's already touched upon in this act, but I feel like this act is a perfect place to include it in. Imagine having a kid one day and wanting to tell then what you did as a kid for fun, but having no way to actually showing them. My first computer was a Commodore 64, and after that an Amiga 500. Sadly, I don't have my Commodore anymore, but I have saved my Amiga! I also have a small kid, and I just need to wait a few more years to be able to show her what computers and video games looked back then, I can't wait for it! Vut this is something that won't be possible with the games of the last decade or two. Major gaming studios have simply decided to kill off their games. These are major corporations now, headed by people who don't care about games at all, it's all just about the money for them. And that's really sad, they are destroying fond memories of many people, and they don't care. There is no reason for them to destroy all these games, preserving them can be done with little to no effort. It's just that they don't care. They would rather spend large amounts of money on legal fees to see this initiative die out, than to spend just a fraction of that money on preserving their legacy. Please help us! We, the common folk, are powerless against them.
